skill_areas.name: Grip Technique
skill_areas.description: Master forehand, backhand, panhandle, and bevel grips. The foundation of every shot in badminton.
pain_points: Holding the racket too tightly (death grip)
Not switching grips between forehand and backhand
Using the same grip for all shots
Incorrect V-grip position on forehand

common_mistakes.mistake: Frying pan grip on all shots
common_mistakes.fix: Practice the V-grip by placing the V of your thumb and index finger on the narrow side of the handle

common_mistakes.mistake: Gripping too tightly throughout the rally
common_mistakes.fix: Relax grip to 30% between shots, tighten to 80% only at contact point

drills: Grip switching drill - alternate forehand/backhand grip 50 times without looking
Wall rally with conscious grip changes
Shadow swing with grip focus using a mirror

progression_path.description: Once grips feel natural, move to serving and basic footwork.

skill_areas.name: Serving
skill_areas.description: Master low serve, high serve, flick serve, and drive serve techniques. The serve is the only shot you have full control over.
pain_points: Inconsistent toss height on high serve
Serving into the net on low serves
Lack of deception on flick serves
Illegal serves (above waist height)
Predictable serve patterns

common_mistakes.mistake: Low serve going too high over the net
common_mistakes.fix: Focus on pushing the shuttle rather than hitting it. Keep racket face open and use a gentle push motion.

common_mistakes.mistake: Always serving to the same spot
common_mistakes.fix: Practice serving to all four corners of the service box. Mix low, flick, and drive serves.

common_mistakes.mistake: Body position giving away serve type
common_mistakes.fix: Keep the same stance and arm position for all serve types. Only change at the last moment.

drills: Wall target practice for low serves - place a target 1 inch above net height
Shadow serving with shuttle placement markers on the court
Alternate low and flick serves to build deception
Serve and return drill with partner - immediate rally after serve
Pressure serving - serve under match-like pressure with scoring

progression_path.description: After mastering basic serves, learn clears to build rallies and footwork for court coverage.

skill_areas.name: Footwork
skill_areas.description: Court coverage using split step, chasse step, lunge, and recovery steps. Good footwork is the difference between average and great players.
pain_points: Slow recovery to base position
Flat-footed stance missing the split step
Poor balance during lunges
Not using chasse steps efficiently
Running instead of shuffling to the shuttle
Landing on wrong foot after overhead shots

common_mistakes.mistake: Not doing a split step before every shot
common_mistakes.fix: Train yourself to do a small hop-split as your opponent contacts the shuttle. This primes your legs for any direction.

common_mistakes.mistake: Crossing legs during lateral movement
common_mistakes.fix: Use side-shuffle (chasse steps) to move laterally. Never cross your feet on court.

common_mistakes.mistake: Not recovering to base position after each shot
common_mistakes.fix: After every shot, push back to center court. Don't admire your shot.

drills: 6-corner footwork shadow drill (3 minutes non-stop)
Ladder agility drills for quick feet
Split step reaction drill with partner calling directions
Lunge and recovery drill - lunge to all 4 corners and push back
Court sprint drill - touch all 4 corners and center in sequence
Shadow badminton - full rally simulation without shuttle

progression_path.description: Once footwork is automatic, you can focus on shot quality because you'll reach the shuttle in good position.

skill_areas.name: Clear Shots
skill_areas.description: Master the high defensive clear and attacking flat clear. Clears are the most fundamental rally-building shot.
pain_points: Shuttle not reaching the back of the court
Using too much arm and not enough body rotation
Poor contact point (too low or too far behind)
Not differentiating between high and flat clear

common_mistakes.mistake: Hitting with a bent arm instead of full extension
common_mistakes.fix: Reach up and forward. Contact the shuttle at the highest possible point with a fully extended arm.

common_mistakes.mistake: No body rotation - using only the arm
common_mistakes.fix: Turn your body sideways, rotate hips and shoulders into the shot. Power comes from rotation, not arm strength.

drills: Continuous clear rally with partner - aim for 30+ consecutive clears
Clear to target zones on the back line
Alternate high clear and flat clear drill
Clear from deep backhand corner drill

progression_path.description: Once clears are consistent, add drop shots and smashes to create an attacking game from the rear court.

skill_areas.name: Smashing
skill_areas.description: Develop powerful overhead smashes including jump smash, stick smash, and half smash. The primary attacking weapon in badminton.
pain_points: Poor wrist snap leading to weak smashes
Incorrect footwork and body rotation
Hitting the frame instead of sweet spot
Lack of angle variation
Smashing from a poor position and losing the rally
No difference between smash and clear preparation

common_mistakes.mistake: Trying to smash every shuttle
common_mistakes.fix: Only smash when the shuttle is above your head and you're balanced. A well-placed drop from a poor position beats a weak smash.

common_mistakes.mistake: Aiming straight at the opponent
common_mistakes.fix: Aim for the body (hard to defend) or sharp angles to the sidelines. Vary your targets.

common_mistakes.mistake: No pronation in the forearm
common_mistakes.fix: The last burst of power comes from forearm pronation (rotation). Practice pronation drills with a towel.

drills: Multi-shuttle smash feeding drill (coach feeds 10-15 shuttles continuously)
Jump smash with target cones in opposite court
Shadow footwork for rear court positioning before smash
Half-smash placement drill - smash at 70% power to specific zones
Smash and follow to net drill - smash then move forward for the return
Backhand smash from rear court drill

progression_path.description: After developing a reliable smash, learn deception to disguise it and net play to finish points.

skill_areas.name: Drop Shots
skill_areas.description: Execute deceptive slow and fast drop shots from the rear court. Drops create openings by pulling opponents to the front.
pain_points: Telegraphing the drop shot with body language
Shuttle going too high over the net
Inconsistent placement
Only using one type of drop (slow or fast)
Drop shots landing mid-court instead of near the net

common_mistakes.mistake: Different preparation for drop vs clear/smash
common_mistakes.fix: Use the same overhead preparation for all rear court shots. Only change at the point of contact.

common_mistakes.mistake: Hitting the shuttle too flat on drops
common_mistakes.fix: Slice across the shuttle to create a steeper trajectory. The shuttle should tumble over the net.

drills: Alternate clear and drop from rear court with partner
Target zone placement drill - place shuttles in 3 drop zones
Deception practice with partner trying to read your shot
Fast drop vs slow drop alternation drill
Cross-court drop shot drill from both forehand and backhand

progression_path.description: Combine drops with deception skills and net play to create a complete front-court game.

skill_areas.name: Net Play
skill_areas.description: Tumbling net shots, net kills, cross-net shots, and net lifts. Net play wins or loses points at the front court.
pain_points: Touching the net during play (fault)
Shuttle spinning incorrectly on tumble shots
Late arrival at the net
Lifting the shuttle too high from the net
Not taking the shuttle early enough

common_mistakes.mistake: Swinging too hard at net shots
common_mistakes.fix: Net shots require soft hands. Use fingers and a gentle push, not a swing. Less is more at the net.

common_mistakes.mistake: Standing too close to the net
common_mistakes.fix: Position yourself about one arm-length from the net. This gives space to react and avoid net faults.

drills: Net shot and lift rally drill with partner
Reaction net kill practice - partner feeds random lifts
Cross-net shot with feeder - alternate straight and cross
Tumbling net shot drill - 50 repetitions for consistency
Net-to-net rally - both players only at the net

progression_path.description: Strong net play combined with deception makes you lethal at the front. Essential for doubles.

skill_areas.name: Defensive Play
skill_areas.description: Handle smash defense with blocks, drives, lifts, and counter-attacks. Good defense turns your opponent's attack into your opportunity.
pain_points: Panic under fast smashes
Weak backhand defense
Poor positioning for doubles defense
Always lifting the shuttle giving away the attack
Not reading the smasher's body language

common_mistakes.mistake: Standing upright when defending
common_mistakes.fix: Get low with knees bent and racket up in front of your body. Lower center of gravity = faster reaction.

common_mistakes.mistake: Big backswing on defense
common_mistakes.fix: Defense requires compact strokes. Use the incoming pace of the smash and redirect it. No backswing needed.

common_mistakes.mistake: Always blocking straight back
common_mistakes.fix: Mix up cross-court blocks, drives to the body, and counter-drops to keep the attacker guessing.

drills: Continuous smash defense drill - partner smashes 20 shots, you defend
Backhand block to target zones on the court
Doubles flat defense rotation drill with 3-4 players
Cross-court defense block practice
Counter-attack drill - block then immediately move to attack

progression_path.description: Solid defense feeds into strategic play in both singles and doubles.

skill_areas.name: Drive Shots
skill_areas.description: Fast, flat shots that travel horizontally over the net. Essential in doubles for fast exchanges and creating pressure.
pain_points: Drives going into the net
Not enough speed on the drive
Poor timing leading to mis-hits
Can't sustain fast drive rallies

common_mistakes.mistake: Using full arm swing on drives
common_mistakes.fix: Drives use compact forearm and wrist action. Short, quick motions generate speed without sacrificing control.

common_mistakes.mistake: Hitting drives from below the net tape
common_mistakes.fix: Take the shuttle early at the highest point possible. Step forward into the shot.

drills: Straight drive rally with partner - sustain for 1 minute
Cross-court drive exchange drill
Drive and net shot combination drill
Reaction drive drill - random feeds from partner

progression_path.description: Drives are crucial for doubles. Combine with doubles strategy for effective partnership play.

skill_areas.name: Deception & Trick Shots
skill_areas.description: Disguise your shots to wrong-foot opponents. Includes reverse slices, hold shots, fake smashes, and deceptive net play.
pain_points: Deception attempts are too obvious
Losing power/accuracy when attempting deception
Not reading when to use deception vs direct shots
Over-using deception and becoming predictable

common_mistakes.mistake: Trying deception every shot
common_mistakes.fix: Deception works because you have established a pattern. Hit 3-4 honest shots, then deceive on the 5th.

common_mistakes.mistake: Body language not matching the fake shot
common_mistakes.fix: The whole body must sell the fake. Your movement, shoulder, arm should all say 'smash' before you play a drop.

drills: Reverse slice drop from smash preparation
Hold and flick at the net
Fake smash to drop with partner reading drill
Cross-court deceptive drop shot practice
Watch and replicate pro deception from match videos

progression_path.description: Deception is a tactical weapon. Combine with match strategy and mental toughness for complete game.

skill_areas.name: Singles Strategy & Tactics
skill_areas.description: Rally building, court geometry, reading opponents, when to attack vs defend, and pacing in singles matches.
pain_points: Playing without a game plan
Not adapting strategy against different opponents
Getting stuck in defensive patterns
Poor shot selection under pressure
Not using the full court width

common_mistakes.mistake: Always hitting to the same area
common_mistakes.fix: Use the four corners and body shots. Move your opponent diagonally to create the maximum running distance.

common_mistakes.mistake: Attacking from a weak position
common_mistakes.fix: If you're off-balance or behind the shuttle, play a clear or high lift to reset. Attack only from a strong position.

drills: Pattern play - clear-drop-net routine with partner
Court geometry drill - hit to maximum distance from opponent
2-on-1 drill - one player defends against two to build court coverage
Match play with specific tactical restrictions (e.g., no smash game)
Video analysis of your own matches for tactical review

progression_path.description: With tactics in place, mental toughness and fitness determine who wins close matches.

skill_areas.name: Doubles Strategy & Formation
skill_areas.description: Attack-defense formations, rotation, communication, serving strategy, and role specialization in doubles.
pain_points: Poor rotation between front and back
Confusion on who takes mid-court shots
Inability to maintain attacking formation
No communication with partner
Weak third-shot strategy after serve

common_mistakes.mistake: Both players standing side-by-side when attacking
common_mistakes.fix: When attacking, one player should be at the front (intercepting) and one at the back (smashing). Only go side-by-side when defending.

common_mistakes.mistake: Not calling shots in the middle
common_mistakes.fix: Establish clear rules: forehand player takes middle shots, or the player who's 'hotter' calls it. Communicate loudly.

common_mistakes.mistake: Serving too high in doubles
common_mistakes.fix: In doubles, the low serve is king. Keep it tight and just over the net tape. Mix with occasional flick.

drills: Rotation drill with coach feeding to different areas
Attack-defense pattern play with 4 players
Communication and calling practice during rally
Serve and third shot drill - serve, partner moves, execute plan
Mixed doubles specific rotation drill

progression_path.description: Refined doubles play requires mental coordination with your partner and fast drive exchanges.

skill_areas.name: Warm-Up & Pre-Match Routines
skill_areas.description: Dynamic stretching, joint mobilization, and gradual intensity warm-up before playing. Prevents injury and improves performance.
pain_points: Skipping warm-up and getting injured
Static stretching before play (harmful)
Not warming up the wrist and shoulder specifically
Going 100% intensity immediately

common_mistakes.mistake: Static stretching as warm-up
common_mistakes.fix: Do dynamic stretches before play (leg swings, arm circles, lunges). Save static stretching for after play.

common_mistakes.mistake: Ignoring wrist warm-up
common_mistakes.fix: Badminton puts huge stress on wrists. Do wrist circles, flexion/extension, and light swings before playing.

drills: 5-minute dynamic warm-up routine: jog, high knees, butt kicks, lateral shuffles
Joint mobilization: ankles, knees, hips, shoulders, wrists (30 seconds each)
Shadow footwork at increasing intensity
Easy rally warm-up - clears and drops at 50% speed
Gradual intensity serving practice

progression_path.description: A proper warm-up routine leads naturally into footwork drills and fitness training.

skill_areas.name: Badminton-Specific Fitness
skill_areas.description: Cardiovascular endurance, leg strength, core stability, wrist strength, and agility training tailored for badminton.
pain_points: Running out of stamina in long matches
Legs giving out in the third game
Weak wrist leading to injuries
Slow reaction time
Poor explosive power for jump smashes

common_mistakes.mistake: Only doing long-distance running for cardio
common_mistakes.fix: Badminton is interval-based. Train with intervals: 20-sec sprints, 10-sec rest. HIIT is more sport-specific.

common_mistakes.mistake: Ignoring core training
common_mistakes.fix: A strong core transfers power from legs to racket. Planks, Russian twists, and medicine ball throws build core strength.

drills: HIIT circuit: 20s burpees, 20s jump lunges, 20s mountain climbers, 10s rest x 8 rounds
Leg strength: squats, lunges, calf raises (3 sets x 15 reps)
Core circuit: plank 60s, side plank 30s each, bicycle crunches 20 reps
Wrist strengthening: wrist curls with light dumbbell, rice bucket exercises
Agility ladder drills: in-out, lateral, Icky shuffle
Court sprint intervals: shuttle run for 6 corners

progression_path.description: Better fitness directly improves your on-court performance in all areas.

skill_areas.name: Mental Toughness & Match Psychology
skill_areas.description: Handling pressure, staying focused, managing emotions, building confidence, and developing a champion's mindset.
pain_points: Choking at match point
Losing focus after making errors
Getting angry and playing worse
Fear of losing to weaker opponents
Not being able to come back from a deficit

common_mistakes.mistake: Dwelling on the last point
common_mistakes.fix: Each point is independent. Use a reset routine: take a breath, bounce on your toes, ready position. The last point is gone.

common_mistakes.mistake: Changing strategy when losing
common_mistakes.fix: If your strategy is sound, stick with it. Often you're losing because of execution, not strategy. Stay patient.

drills: Pressure serving drill: must make 5 in a row or restart
Practice point scenarios: play from 18-20 down
Breathing exercises between points
Visualization: mentally rehearse match scenarios before sleep
Post-match journaling: write 3 things you did well, 1 to improve

progression_path.description: Mental toughness enhances all tactical aspects of your game.

skill_areas.name: Recovery & Injury Prevention
skill_areas.description: Post-match recovery, stretching, common badminton injuries (shoulder, knee, ankle), and preventive care.
pain_points: Shoulder pain from overhead shots
Knee pain from lunging
Ankle sprains from quick direction changes
Wrist tendinitis
General muscle soreness affecting next session

common_mistakes.mistake: Not cooling down after matches
common_mistakes.fix: Walk for 5 minutes, then do static stretches for all major muscle groups. Hold each stretch 20-30 seconds.

common_mistakes.mistake: Playing through pain
common_mistakes.fix: Distinguish between muscle fatigue and joint pain. Fatigue is normal; sharp joint pain means stop and consult a physio.

drills: Post-match static stretching routine (10 minutes)
Foam rolling for legs, back, and shoulders
Ice bath or cold shower for legs after intense sessions
Rotator cuff strengthening exercises (resistance band)
Ankle stability exercises: single-leg balance, wobble board
Rest day active recovery: light swimming or yoga

progression_path.description: Good recovery enables better fitness training and proper warm-up habits.

skill_areas.name: Equipment Knowledge
skill_areas.description: Understanding racket specs, string tension, shuttlecock types, shoe selection, and when to upgrade equipment.
pain_points: Buying the wrong racket for your level
Not understanding string tension impact
Using worn-out shoes with no grip
Spending too much on equipment too early

common_mistakes.mistake: Buying a pro-level stiff racket as a beginner
common_mistakes.fix: Beginners need flexible shaft rackets. Stiff shafts require advanced technique to generate power.

common_mistakes.mistake: Stringing at very high tension as a beginner
common_mistakes.fix: Start at 22-24 lbs. High tension reduces the sweet spot. Only increase as your technique improves.

drills: Try different racket weights at a sports store before buying
Experiment with different string tensions (2 lbs apart) to feel the difference
Compare nylon and feather shuttlecocks to understand the difference
Check shoe grip regularly - replace when the sole pattern is worn

progression_path.description: Once you have the right equipment, focus on grip and serving fundamentals.

beginner.description: New players should build a solid foundation in grip, basic strokes, serving, and court movement. Get the right equipment and always warm up.

intermediate.description: Players with basic rally ability should develop attacking shots, deception, and defensive skills. Start fitness training and learn recovery.

advanced.description: Competitive players should refine deception, tactical awareness, doubles coordination, and mental toughness.

aggressive_player.description: You love attacking and finishing points quickly. Your weapons are smash, jump smash, and fast drops.
play_style_tips: Develop a threatening smash that opponents fear
Use clears to push opponents deep, then punish short returns
Work on fitness - aggressive play demands stamina
Learn when NOT to attack - patience sets up better opportunities

defensive_player.description: You outlast opponents with consistency and solid defense. You wait for mistakes and capitalize.
play_style_tips: Your defense should frustrate attackers into making errors
Develop excellent court coverage with superior footwork
Use high clears to slow the pace and reset rallies
Build exceptional stamina to outlast opponents in long games

tactical_player.description: You read the game like chess. You set traps, use deception, and outthink opponents.
tactical_player.recommended_racket_type: Speed/Control (Head Light or Even, Medium Flex)
play_style_tips: Study your opponent's patterns in the first few rallies
Use deception to create uncertainty - make them guess
Control the pace of the rally - speed up and slow down intentionally
Develop a wide variety of shots so you're never predictable

power_player.description: Raw power is your weapon. You generate pace that opponents struggle to handle.
play_style_tips: Invest in physical strength training for even more power
Learn to use power selectively - not every shot needs maximum force
Develop a punishing jump smash as your signature weapon
Balance power with placement - a well-placed half-smash beats a wild full smash

control_player.description: Precision and placement define your game. You put the shuttle exactly where you want it.
play_style_tips: Your net game should be razor sharp - tumbles that barely clear the tape
Develop pinpoint drop shots to all areas of the front court
Master the serve to start every rally with an advantage
Use slice and spin to make your shots harder to read
